channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Paul McCartney on His New Album and the Beatles’ Earliest Days
item.description: On his new album, the ex-Beatle revisits his past and revels in simply continuing to create.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles
item.media:credit: Jim Dyson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Paul McCartney performing in London in 2024.

item.title: Review: ‘The Maids’ Makes a Ruckus but Not Enough Mess
item.description: Jean Genet’s psychosexual drama gets a social-media-heavy update. But what does it say beyond “internet=bad”?
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lydia Wilson, left, and Phia Saban in “The Maids” at St. Ann’s Warehouse in Brooklyn.

item.title: 3 Weeks After Fire, ‘The Book of Mormon’ Reopens on Broadway
item.description: The satirical musical, which is celebrating its 15th anniversary this year, missed 25 performances during the shutdown.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and Sarah Bahr
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Eugene O’Neill Theater required repair work after an electrical fire.

item.title: What Orchestras and Singers Gain Through Operas in Concert
item.description: The Cleveland Orchestra’s recent performances of Beethoven’s “Fidelio” join concerts that fill a gap in American opera.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Reynolds

item.title: Jane Austen Snubbed This Character. ‘The Other Bennet Sister’ Loves Her.
item.description: As the star of “The Other Bennet Sister,” this actress is redeeming a “Pride and Prejudice” character Austen cast as a tedious prig.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Meghan Marin
item.media:credit: Meghan Marin for The New York Times

item.title: Florentina Holzinger’s 9-Hour Spectacle Pushes the Limits of Body Art
item.description: The choreographer-provocateur followed a hit show at the Venice Biennale with one in Vienna. “Violence is something we’re used to,” she said.
item.dc:creator: Valeriya Safronova

item.title: ‘Backrooms’ Review: Lost in the Expansion
item.description: Born from a single unsettling image, this viral horror concept from Kane Parsons wears thin under the weight of a full feature.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: Firelei Báez Harvests Trickster Energy to Remap Stories of the World
item.description: In her new exhibition, the Dominican painter retooled graphics from the past to reimagine the future.
item.dc:creator: J Wortham and Guarionex Rodriguez
item.media:credit: Firelei Báez/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York
item.media:description: Firelei Báez with a towering bronze sculpture with feathers titled “Ayida,” a centerpiece in her exhibition at Hauser & Wirth. It represents a ciguepas, a female trickster of Dominican folklore (equal parts woman, plant and animal) that is a powerful shape-shifter, always evolving.

item.title: For Rent: A One-Bedroom Coyote House
item.description: The artist Johnny DeFeo reimagined his wildlife paintings as a tiny adventure house that he constructed high in the New Mexican desert. (Vacationers, check Airbnb.)
item.dc:creator: Ray Mark Rinaldi

item.title: Thornton Wilder’s Last Play Vanished Into Thin Air. Or Did It?
item.description: Decades after “The Emporium” failed to open on Broadway in 1954, one man went on a quest to find it.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green
item.category: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library
item.media:credit: Fred Stein Archive/Archive Photos via Getty Images
item.media:description: Thornton Wilder won three Pulitzer Prizes, including one for the classic drama “Our Town,” still among the most produced plays in the United States.

item.title: Tiler Peck’s New Ballet Expands the Idea of What Female and Male Dancers Can Be
item.description: The idea of sisterhood and brotherhood flows through “Symphonie Espagnole,” Peck’s new work for New York City Ballet. We dissect two sections.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas
item.media:credit: Rachel Papo for The New York Times

item.title: Matthew Perry’s Assistant, Kenneth Iwamasa, Sentenced to More Than 3 Years in Prison
item.description: Kenneth Iwamasa injected Mr. Perry with the ketamine that killed him. He is the last of five defendants to be sentenced in the case.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Willy Sanjuan/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The actor Matthew Perry was found unresponsive in a hot tub at his home in Los Angeles on Oct. 28, 2023. His assistant had injected him with at least three shots of ketamine that day.

item.title: The ‘Becky Shaw’ Star Who ‘Does Horrible Things’ Oh So Well
item.description: The Tony nominee Alden Ehrenreich has been making audiences cry at this Broadway comedy in which he portrays a cynical money manager.
item.dc:creator: Melena Ryzik
item.media:credit: Brian Karlsson for The New York Times

item.title: Blake Lively’s Last Chance: A U.S. Judge’s Take on an Untested State Law
item.description: After the settlement with Justin Baldoni’s company, one potentially thorny legal issue remains: whether she is due damages under a new California law.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz/Reuters
item.media:description: Blake Lively settled her retaliation claims that stemmed from the making of the 2024 movie “It Ends with Us,” but she is continuing to seek compensation through another legal avenue.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Finds Glee in President Trump’s Approval Rating
item.description: A new poll shows it sitting at 34 percent. “He has the same approval rating as ‘Paul Blart: Mall Cop,’” Kimmel said on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: ‘Hacks’ Leaves Them Laughing
item.description: In an interview, Jean Smart and Hannah Einbinder discuss the series finale, their offscreen relationship and why comedy is just like sex.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Chantal Anderson
item.media:credit: Chantal Anderson for The New York Times

item.title: Curating in the Cross Hairs: Is This the Smithsonian Chief’s Last Show?
item.description: Lonnie G. Bunch III, the Smithsonian secretary under pressure from the White House, organized an exhibit exploring America’s founding ideals.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I love thinking creatively about, how do you protect the Smithsonian?” said Lonnie G. Bunch III, the institution’s secretary. “How do you make sure issues of scholarship and integrity are there?”

item.title: Young MC and Morris Day Pull Out of Freedom 250 Concerts
item.description: Young MC, Martina McBride and Morris Day were among those who said they would not perform at the series on the National Mall.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Abrams
item.media:credit: Rob Grabowski/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: “I have informed my agents that I will not be performing,” the rapper Young MC said about Freedom 250.

item.title: Totó la Momposina, Colombian Folk Music Star, Dies at 85
item.description: Timbaland, Jay-Z and Major Lazer sampled her work, and she received a lifetime achievement award from the Latin Grammys in 2013.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Goodman
item.category: Latin Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences
item.media:credit: C Brandon/Redferns, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Totó in 2015. “We want to help our country’s music express the culture of all our people,” she said.

item.title: ‘Power Ballad’ Review: Hitting All the Wrong Notes
item.description: There are some promising themes in this comedy-drama about an American musician in Dublin, featuring Nick Jonas and Paul Rudd, but the movie never develops them.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Three Great Documentaries to Stream
item.description: This month’s picks explore the French legal system, a neighborhood conflict in Florida and the cinema legacy of the Lumière brothers.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg
item.media:credit: Icarus Films/Distrib Films
item.media:description: In “12 Days,” the director Raymond Depardon every so often folds in exterior images among the scenes of judges interviewing psychiatric patients.

item.title: ‘Pressure’ Review: The Weather of War
item.description: Amid the chaos of World War II, this dull film centers on dueling weather reports in the period leading up to D-Day.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘With Hasan in Gaza’ Review: An Elusive Search
item.description: The Palestinian filmmaker Kamal Aljafari has constructed this elliptical documentary out of material filmed in Gaza in 2001, when he was searching for a friend.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Propeller One-Way Night Coach’: John Travolta’s Directing Debut
item.description: John Travolta makes his directing debut with this short and sentimental coming-of-age film about a son’s first plane trip taken with his single mother.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Miss You, Love You’ Review: A Son Outsources His Solace
item.description: The mourning process feels theatrical in this dramedy starring Allison Janney as a new widow estranged from her son.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘The Last Viking’ Review: Imagine Mads Mikkelsen
item.description: Mikkelsen plays a man convinced he’s John Lennon in a darkly comic crime film from the Danish auteur Anders Thomas Jensen.
item.dc:creator: Calum Marsh

item.title: ‘Forastera’ Review: An Exquisitely Deconstructed Ghost Story
item.description: A teenager affects aspects of her late grandmother in this beguiling first feature set on the island of Mallorca.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: ‘The Breadwinner’ Review: Nate Bargatze, Man of the House
item.description: The comedian makes a big swing with this movie about a stay-at-home dad, and gets a big assist from three funny kids.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘The Currents’ Review: Lost and Found
item.description: Milagros Mumenthaler’s superb, sensuously realized film follows an Argentine fashion stylist trying to overcome a deep sense of destabilization.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: The Real Story Behind “Pressure,” the Film About the D-Day Weather Forecast
item.description: The new film “Pressure” tells the story of the fateful D-Day weather forecast. Here’s what it got right and wrong from the historical record.
item.dc:creator: Nazaneen Ghaffar

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Can Hardly Believe the Reality of L.A.’s Mayoral Race
item.description: “Mayor should not be your first job,” the host said of the former reality show star and mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt, who is polling in second place.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: David Henderson, Innovative Poet and Hendrix Biographer, Dies at 83
item.description: Part of the Black Arts Movement of the 1960s, he went on to reclaim a leading musician of the psychedelic era as a distinctly African American artist.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Add Some Joy to Your Day
item.description: Aggression cookies, dancing the seasons and typing ‘Mrs. Dalloway.’
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: This Year at the May Festival, Julia Bullock Took Charge
item.description: The May Festival in Cincinnati was led this time by the soprano Julia Bullock, who wove her experiences as a Black artist through two weeks of concerts.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy Reynolds
item.media:credit: Madeleine Hordinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: Julia Bullock at Music Hall in Cincinnati.

item.title: My Starring Role in ‘Heated Rivalry’ — for One Night Only
item.description: The unauthorized musical parody of the hit TV show gives the role of Scott Hunter to an unsuspecting audience member — and one recent night that was me.
item.dc:creator: Tim Teeman
item.category: Heated Rivalry: The Unauthorized Musical Parody (Play)
item.media:credit: Matthew Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jay Armstrong Johnson, left, as Ilya Rozanov and Jimin Moon as Shane Hollander in the musical spoof of “Heated Rivalry” at the Culture Club in Manhattan.

item.title: How to Find Tickets to the 2026 Tony Award-Nominated Plays and Musicals
item.description: Many of the top contenders are onstage right now. Here’s a guide to help you navigate the field and find tickets.
item.dc:creator: Laura Collins-Hughes
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: “The Lost Boys” received 12 Tony nominations, including one for Ali Louis Bourzgui’s performance as David, the lead singer of a rock band who also happens to be a vampire.

item.title: The ‘Yogurt Shop Murders’ Case Was Solved, but the Story Hasn’t Changed
item.description: The true culprit was identified soon after this HBO documentary came out. A new episode shows that the series was less about who did the killings than about the agony of uncertainty.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale

item.title: Bringing Courbet’s ‘A Burial at Ornans’ Back to Life, While Visitors Watch
item.description: The Musée d’Orsay demystified art conservation by turning the meticulous, yearlong restoration of a 22-foot Gustave Courbet painting into a public event.
item.dc:creator: Elaine Sciolino and Elliott Verdier
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times

item.title: The Cast of ‘I Love Boosters’ Want You to Think Critically
item.description: Four cast members from Boots Riley’s working-class comedy discuss capitalism, art, protest and trying to change the world (with some laughs thrown in).
item.dc:creator: Sandra E. Garcia and Dana Scruggs
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: Why Is TikTok in This Book from 2006?
item.description: For decades, publishers have swapped out cultural references in new editions of books to appeal to younger readers. Fans aren’t always thrilled.
item.dc:creator: Angelina Mazza
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: The $400 Million Showdown Between a Billionaire and a California Mayor
item.description: The owner of the Los Angeles Rams and the City of Inglewood are in a dispute over Hollywood Park and SoFi Stadium, which is about to host World Cup matches.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens, Ken Belson and Emmanuel Morgan
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Inglewood, Calif., argues that its 2015 development agreement with Hollywood Park, which includes SoFi Stadium, is void because of a subsequent state court decision.

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Slams Trump for Missing His Son’s Wedding
item.description: “It was a small ceremony with about 40 guests, none of whom were his father,” the late-night host said of Donald Trump Jr.’s wedding in the Bahamas over the weekend.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix

item.title: 4 Takeaways From the ‘Summer House’ Season 10 Reunion
item.description: The highly anticipated episode delved into the timeline of the relationship between Amanda Batula and West Wilson and the pair’s confrontation with Ciara Miller and the rest of their housemates.
item.dc:creator: Shivani Gonzalez
item.media:credit: Clifton Prescod/Bravo
item.media:description: Kyle Cooke and Ciara Miller on the “Summer House” Season 10 reunion.

item.title: ‘Chess’ Will Close With Lea Michele’s Departure, in a Change of Plans
item.description: The Broadway musical had planned to keep going, but box-office sales made clear Michele’s appeal was essential. It will now close June 21.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Lea Michele in the revival of “Chess” now running at Broadway’s Imperial Theater.

item.title: How Cannes Is Grappling With Changes
item.description: Reporting from the Cannes Film Festival, our film critic Alissa Wilkinson describes how the event is both fending off and embracing aspects of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson and Edward Vega

item.title: 5 Great Songs I Encountered This Week
item.description: Hear Mick Taylor’s swan song, Grace Ives’s exuberant crushing and a foundational hip-hop sample.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: J. Wilds/Hulton Archive, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Rolling Stones in 1969: From left, Charlie Watts, Mick Taylor, Mick Jagger, Keith Richards and Bill Wyman.

item.title: John Barbour, Who Played Key Role in the Rise of Reality TV, Dies at 93
item.description: He was a creator and producer of “Real People,” a hit NBC series about eccentrics and unusual lifestyles, which spawned imitators like “That’s Incredible!”
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:credit: NBCUniversal, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John Barbour in 1981. Mr. Barbour was a producer and writer of the good-natured series “Real People,” bantering cheerfully about its subjects with the members of an onscreen panel.

item.title: Ben Morea, Principal Provocateur of 1960s Anarchist Group, Dies at 84
item.description: An abstract painter who wanted to creatively destroy the gap between art and revolution, he loosely led a band of radicals with a profane name.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald
item.media:credit: Tony Cenicola/The New York Times
item.media:description: Ben Morea in 2016 at an exhibition of his paintings in New York City. He was mostly known for an anarchist-centered activism he called “armed love.”

item.title: Julia Louis-Dreyfus to Star in ‘Other Desert Cities’ on Broadway
item.description: The revival, also featuring Ed Harris, Allison Janney, Lily Rabe and Joe Keery, will begin performances in September at the Hudson Theater.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Thibaud Moritz/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Louis-Dreyfus, photographed earlier this month at the Cannes film festival, will be making her Broadway debut in the play.

item.title: L.A. Philharmonic Names Daniel Harding as Music Director
item.description: The Philharmonic, taking a more traditional route than in the past, has named the British-born conductor Daniel Harding as its next music director.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: Benjamin Malapris for The New York Times
item.media:description: The conductor Daniel Harding, who will begin as the music director of the Los Angeles Philharmonic in the 2027-28 season.

item.title: ‘The Maids,’ With Yerin Ha, Asks: Has Life Become One Big Performance?
item.description: In his rethinking of Jean Genet’s classic work about class and power, Kip Williams ponders “a world that gives you every opportunity not to be yourself.”
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas
item.media:credit: Richard Termine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Power dynamic: Saban and Ha in Kip Williams’s production, which was rewritten in a contemporary idiom while hewing closely to Genet’s plot.

item.title: Opera Philadelphia: Behind Its Comeback
item.description: Anthony Roth Costanzo took over Opera Philadelphia when it was “three weeks away from stopping payroll.” Now, it is rebounding with a surplus.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times

item.title: ‘Armageddon’: The Blockbuster Melodrama That Left Our Heads Spinning
item.description: Michael Bay wasn’t remotely worried about the science in his 1998 science-fiction action hit that launched an elite ensemble cast to space.
item.dc:creator: Maya Salam

item.title: Celebrity Assistants Exist to Indulge Their Bosses, but When Does Duty Cross a Line?
item.description: Matthew Perry’s assistant injected the ketamine that killed his employer. His sentencing has some in the demanding profession considering the power dynamics involved.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:credit: Raymond Hall/GC Images, via Getty Images
item.media:description: An autopsy report attributed Matthew Perry’s death in 2023 to the “acute effects" of ketamine. The actor’s personal assistant had found him unresponsive in a hot tub in his home in Los Angeles.

item.title: Sonny Rollins: 12 Essential Albums
item.description: The towering saxophonist, who died at 95, was a master of living in the moment. Listen to some of his most compelling work, onstage and in the studio.
item.dc:creator: Hank Shteamer
item.media:credit: Bruno Bebert/European Pressphoto Agency
item.media:description: Sonny Rollins performing in Nice, France, in 2009.

item.title: Sonny Rollins, Giant of the Jazz Saxophone, Is Dead at 95
item.description: Even by the standards of a music that prizes individuality, he stood out, as both a musician and a personality.
item.dc:creator: Peter Keepnews
item.media:credit: Stephanie Berger for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sonny Rollins in 2006. He flirted with the avant-garde, jazz-rock fusion and other styles over the years, but he was ultimately unclassifiable.

item.title: Lizzo Addresses the Backlash and Performs Live at The New York Times
item.description: During a special edition of “Popcast,” the singer discussed her new album and the public fallout from a series of lawsuits. Then she performed a stripped-down set.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli and Jon Caramanica
item.media:credit: Thea Traff for The New York Times

item.title: Ryan Speedo Green in ‘Die Walküre’: Preview of the Met Opera
item.description: Ryan Speedo Green has been previewing his take on Wotan, ruler of the gods, with Gustavo Dudamel and the Los Angeles Philharmonic.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Asher
item.media:description: Ryan Speedo Green, front, as Wotan in “Die Walküre,” near Christine Goerke as Brünnhilde.

item.title: How Art Auctions Choreographed a $2.5 Billion Comeback
item.description: After four years of uneven sales, the auction houses engineered a successful season by redefining the expectations of buyers and sellers—and avoiding flops.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small, Julia Halperin and Tim F. Schneider
item.media:credit: Pollock-Krasner Foundation/Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York; Photo by George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jackson Pollock, “Number 7A” (1948), a horizontal drip painting, an example of the artist’s “action painting” style from a peak period, is a highlight of the Newhouse collection sale at Christie’s that fetched $181.2 million including fees.

item.title: ‘Spider-Noir’ Is a Multicolor Spin on ‘Spider-Man’
item.description: The new Spider-series stars Nicolas Cage as a hard-boiled investigator and former web slinger. It comes in both black-and-white and color versions.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman
item.media:credit: Aaron Epstein/Prime Video
item.media:description: “Spider-Noir,” with Nicolas Cage, channels modern comic books and pulpy Hollywood films of the past.

item.title: The International Dance League Wants to Be the ‘NBA of Dance’
item.description: The International Dance League wants to give dancers a career path and to create stars. “Commercialization is good,” said a founder of the league.
item.dc:creator: Margaret Fuhrer and Shuran Huang
item.media:description: GRV, from Los Angeles, is another of the league’s six founding teams.
